summary: The session focused on the diversity of bird species in Yellowstone National Park, highlighting that over 300 species have been reported, with several being endangered. The user expressed concern about endangered species and inquired about conservation efforts in the park.
facts: More than 300 species of birds have been reported in Yellowstone National Park. The most commonly seen birds in Yellowstone National Park include the American dipper, mountain bluebird, osprey, bald eagle, common raven, and various species of woodpeckers, warblers, and finches. The trumpeter swan and peregrine falcon are considered threatened in Yellowstone National Park. The bald eagle and whooping crane are classified as endangered in Yellowstone National Park. The Yellowstone population of the gray-crowned rosy finch is considered rare and a significant conservation concern. Yellowstone National Park has programs and initiatives in place to protect and conserve bird species. The park collaborates with state and federal agencies, private organizations, and universities on bird research, monitoring, and management efforts. Yellowstone National Park is committed to restoring and protecting crucial habitats for endangered bird species throughout the park. Yellowstone National Park regularly monitors bird populations and conducts research to understand how different species are affected by environmental changes. The park offers educational programs for visitors to learn about the importance of preserving and conserving endangered bird species.
